div.p: Why r10 instead of rcx for arg 3? The syscall instruction (x86_64) uses rcx to save the return address (RIP) before switching to kernel mode. rcx is destroyed. The kernel ABI therefore uses r10 for arg 3. The C function ABI uses rcx for arg 4 — these are DIFFERENT conventions. Using regs.rcx for syscall arg 3 returns the saved RIP value, not the argument. This bug is silent.

p.strong: ABI Comparison: syscall vs C function

tr.td: syscall saves RIP → rcx; sysret restores from rcx+r11.
Kernel therefore cannot use rcx for argument passing.

div.p: open() args[0] = ARG_STR args[1] = ARG_OPEN_FLAGS args[2] = ARG_UINT nargs = 3
mmap() args[0] = ARG_PTR args[1] = ARG_UINT args[2] = ARG_MMAP_PROT args[3] = ARG_MMAP_FLAGS args[4] = ARG_FD args[5] = ARG_UINT nargs = 6
syscall_info_t syscall_nr ← orig_rax args[0] ← rdi args[1] ← rsi args[2] ← rdx args[3] ← r10 ✓ args[4] ← r8 args[5] ← r9 retval ← rax (exit)
